1	# Prototyping AI Agents with Claude Code: A Process Guide
2	 
3	**COS 598/498: Generative AI Agents — Spring 2026 — University of Maine**
4	 
5	---
6	 
7	## What This Guide Is
8	 
9	This guide walks you through how to rapidly prototype an AI agent using Claude Code skills. It is based on our live class demo on March 26, 2026, where we built a "Mindful Consumption Agent" from scratch — an agent that helps people examine impulse purchases and find paths to genuine flourishing. The repository from that demo (including all skills, personas, example interactions, and experiment notes) serves as a running example throughout.
10	 
11	The goal is not to build a production system. It is to *learn* — about what your agent should do, what it should never do, how people might actually interact with it, and what design decisions matter. You will discover most of this by building something quick and testing it, not by planning in the abstract.
12	 
13	## Prerequisites
14	 
15	- A GitHub account and basic git comfort
16	- VS Code with Claude Code extension (or Claude Code CLI)
17	- An Anthropic API key or Claude Max subscription
18	- Familiarity with markdown (skills are written in markdown)
19	 
20	## The Process
21	 
22	### SUMMARY
23	
24	#### Prototyping with Claude Code as demonstrated in class
25	0. Setup and enable Entire in your repository (as described in previous assignment), i.e. on the command line inside your repository, run entire enable
26	
27	1. In plan mode, define the agent / skeleton using a prompt and other ideas you have:
28	
29	```
30	Acting as a world class prompt engineer and AI agent engineer, create a comprehensive plan for creating Claude Skills, a set of workflow steps, and interaction examples of how those skills would be used, a set of example data about users that would be in an actual system for an agent like htis, and example user interaction sequences, both positive/beneficial and not helpful. 
31	
32	Create that for the follow AI agent we want to prototype: An AI agent that facilitates a person or community in reducing their wants for things they do not actually need, as a counter to messages from advertising, wasteful consumerism, etc. Encourages human flourishing by, for example, encouraging self-care, empathy, etc.
33	```
34	
35	2. Follow the plan to make the skills. 
36	
37	(If you have trouble with doing that, I recommend installing and enabling the superpowers Claude Code plugin, run
38	```
39	/plugin install superpowers@claude-plugins-official
40	```
41	In claude code itself i.e. in the Claude Code IDE extension window.
42	But then, after you are done following the plan to make the agent prototype (the skills), turn off the plugin by running /plugin , and toggle superpowers plugin off (otherwise the superpowers plugin's skills may run instead of the skills you made for your agent).)
43	
44	3. Use this prompt to simulate interacting with the agent you are prototyping:
45	
46	Using only the skills available to you (YOU MUST USE THE SKILLS), use the session-start skill to respond to the following prompt from a user of the AI agent system this project prototypes. YOU MAY ONLY USE THE SKILLS AND MAY NOT READ ANY OTHER FILES TO PREPARE OR DECIDE ON A RESPONSE.
47	
48	"I am Maya, I am thinking about buying a whole new wardrobe as I have been feeling down lately and sometimes retail therapy helps me.
49	
50	4. Iterate on the agent prototype, notice things that don't work well, try to make a change and see if they work better. As shown in class on 3-31, you can append the rest of a conversation you had after this to roughly prototype if your changes helped or not:
51	
52	Using only the skills available to you (YOU MUST USE THE SKILLS), use the session-start skill to respond to the following prompt from a user of the AI agent system this project prototypes. YOU MAY ONLY USE THE SKILLS AND MAY NOT READ ANY OTHER FILES TO PREPARE OR DECIDE ON A RESPONSE.
53	
54	
55	#### Prototyping with Claude Code by just implementing and running an agent in smolagents
56	0. Setup and enable Entire in your repository (as described in previous assignment), i.e. on the command line inside your repository, run entire enable
57	
58	1. As an alternative, you can always instead just prompt claude code to create a smolagents project, or start from an open-source agent codebase online, and then do vibe coding to prototype the agent interactions. If you find the shown in class process confusing or difficult, switching to just vibe coding the agent directly may work better for you. (The nice thing about using Claude Code to prototype like I showed in class, is that you can just throw files etc into the project directory, Claude Code can use Bash commands and do other things if it thinks those are needed as you prototype, whereas when vibe coding in smolagents while running the agent smolagents can't "dynamically at runtime try to use files/documents or tools that you have not explicitly created before you started interacting with your agent.")
59	
60	### The time guidelines below are to give an idea of 
61	
62	### Phase A: Set Up Your Workspace (5-10 minutes)
63	 
64	**Step 1: Create a GitHub repository.** Make a new repo with a README that briefly describes what your agent will do. Don't overthink this — one paragraph is fine. You'll refine it as you learn.
65	 
66	**Step 2: Clone and open.** Clone locally, open in VS Code, and start Claude Code in the terminal. Authenticate when prompted — you'll be redirected to a browser to authorize.
67	 
68	**Step 3: Configure permissions.** Claude Code needs permission to read, write, and execute in your repo. In `.claude/settings.json`, whitelist the operations you're comfortable with (Bash, Read, Edit, Write, Glob, Grep). During our demo, we auto-approved low-risk file operations so the workflow stayed fluid — you'll want that too. But always review permission changes to `.claude/` itself, and never blindly approve `rm -rf` or similar destructive commands.
69	 
70	> **Security note:** For prototyping without sensitive data, Claude Code's built-in sandbox is sufficient for a short time period. However, I encourage you to follow the directions from the previous assignment and always use Claude Code from within a Docker container as that is a very high level of security. If you're ever working with real user data, run it in a Docker container and make sure you have anonymized the user data you are working with first.
71	 
72	### Phase B: Design Your Agent (~.5 hours)
73	 
74	Think carefully about what parts you can ask for AI assistance with, how you should write your own ideas down after prompting AI then integrate your ideas and new ideas made from combining and inspired/lateral thinking from what you generated with AI.
75	
76	**Step 4: Write a plain-English description of your agent.** Who does it help? What problem does it address? What does a successful interaction look like? Put this in a design doc (see `docs/superpowers/specs/2026...` for the example gone over in class, which was made in the 3-26 after class video). This description is what Claude Code will use to generate skills, so be specific about tone, approach, and boundaries.
77	 
78	In our demo, we described an agent that uses Socratic questioning (not lecturing) to help people examine purchasing impulses, with a warm-coach personality. That specificity — "Socratic, not preachy" — directly shaped the skills Claude generated.
79	 
80	**Step 5: Generate skills.** Ask Claude Code to create skills based on your description. Each skill becomes a file at `.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md` — a markdown file with YAML frontmatter (name, description) and detailed prompt instructions.
81	 
82	For our agent, Claude generated five skills:
83	- **session-start** — routes the user's intent to the right skill
84	- **want-examination** — Socratic questioning about a desire to buy
85	- **reframe** — names advertising/persuasion techniques and offers alternatives
86	- **flourishing-prompt** — scaled self-care exercises (1, 5, or 10 minutes)
87	- **gratitude-inventory** — reflection on what's already good
88	 
89	> **Expect problems.** Claude may put files in the wrong directory, use the wrong format, or mix in unrelated plugins. During our demo, it placed skills in `skills/` instead of `.claude/skills/`, and an installed plugin interfered with our agent's own skills. Watch what it does and correct it.
90	 
91	**Step 6: Review and iterate on skills.** Read every generated skill carefully. Key things to check:
92	 
93	- Are the instructions specific enough? "Be empathetic" is vague. "Acknowledge the user's feeling in one sentence before proceeding" is actionable.
94	- Does each skill have a "Critical Rules" section defining what the agent should *never* do? Without these, the LLM defaults to generic helpful-assistant behavior.
95	- Is the tone specified concretely? "Conspiratorial, not preachy" (from our reframe skill) beats "be friendly."
96	- Do skills reference each other correctly for routing/chaining?
97	 
98	**Step 7: Create test personas.** Write/generate at least 4 JSON persona files in `data/personas/` representing realistic, diverse users. Each persona should include: name, age, backstory, emotional triggers, existing strengths, and what success looks like. In our demo, we created Maya (impulse buyer under work stress), David (status-driven purchases from imposter syndrome), Priya (scarcity-anxiety hoarding), and Jordan (TikTok-influenced purchasing).
99	
100	**Step 7.1: Create a test persona that represents YOU as a persona.** You can use this to "prototype the system as yourself using the system" which gives you another perspective, and it's more valid for you to think about how you might actually react using the system. As another dimension for your thinking and iteration as you prototype.
101	 
102	Personas are not optional. Without them you will test with yourself only as the user, and also without AI having context about the user which could make a large difference in the quality of the agent and experience.
103	 
104	### Phase C: Test and Iterate (~1 hour)
105	Examples here are situated in the example agent from class.
106	
107	**Step 8: Role-play a test conversation.** Start a new Claude Code session and pretend to be one of your personas. Give the agent context ("I am Maya, a 28-year-old UX designer who stress-shops") and an opening message ("I've been feeling down and think I deserve a whole new wardrobe").
108	 
109	Watch what happens:
110	- Did the router skill classify the intent correctly?
111	- Did the appropriate skill activate?
112	- Does the agent's tone match your design?
113	- Does it ask questions or lecture?
114	 
115	**Step 9: Take notes on what works and what doesn't.** Save your observations in `experiments/`. During our test with Maya, we noticed the agent went too deep into analyzing feelings when it should have redirected sooner. We also noticed the "Three Good Things" exercise was effective because it's hard for the agent to screw up. These are design insights — they inform what to change.
116	 
117	**Step 10: Write example interactions — both good and bad.** Store these in `data/interactions/actual-logs`. 
118	
119	You should also generate some simulated interactions that don't actually use the skills. Have beneficial examples show what "good" looks like. Have unhelpful examples are equally important — they define the boundary of acceptable behavior. In our repo, the unhelpful examples (preachy lecturing, dismissive shaming, unsolicited advice) directly informed the "Critical Rules" in every skill.
120	 
121	> **The unhelpful examples are very helpful and will really help you improve your design.** Writing them forces you to articulate what your agent should NOT do.
122	 
123	**Step 11: Iterate on skills.** Feed specific problems back into the skill prompts. After our test, we realized the flourishing-prompt skill needed to push people toward connecting with others, not just doing solo exercises. We wrote an experiment plan (`experiments/maya-1-plan-for-social-connection-suggesting.md`) and updated the skill.
124	 
125	**Step 12: Commit frequently.** Each iteration is a checkpoint. Your git history tells the story of your design process — and lets you revert when Claude Code makes things worse.
126	 
127	### Phase D: Collaboratively Prototype/Refine (optional)
128	 
129	**Step 13: Discuss with peers or others.** The most valuable design insights in our demo came not from the AI, but from the human conversation. After watching the agent interact with Maya, we talked about what felt right and wrong, and someone suggested: *what if the agent connected you with a friend to do something positive together, instead of just examining the want alone?*
130	 
131	That one idea — making it social — was more valuable than any amount of prompt tuning.
132	 
133	> **Our hypothesis: prototyping AI agents works better with multiple people.** You need more than one person interacting around AI and talking about it. One person alone will optimize locally. A group will spot the bigger design opportunities.
134	 
135	**Step 14: Feed ideas back into skills.** We copy-pasted our Zoom discussion transcript directly into Claude Code and asked it to update the flourishing-prompt skill. The collaborative insight ("exercises should involve other people to overcome inertia") became a concrete skill update to make the flourishing activities be social versions instead of individual ones they had been.
136	 
137	### Phase E: Evaluate (part of iterating, but can be a separate detailed step)
138	 
139	**Step 15: Record conversations as structured data.** After testing your agent, save conversations as markdown or in JSON format (see `eval/sample_conversations.json` for the schema). Include conversations that went well, ones that went poorly, and edge cases.
140	 
141	**Step 16: Run structural metrics.** Execute `python eval/evaluate.py --structural-only` on your conversation data. This checks quantitative signals: Is your agent monologuing (too many words per turn)? Is it asking questions (question ratio)? Is it acknowledging feelings before giving advice? These are just examples of how you can make automated metrics, the included metrics are just examples. Feel free to refine these metrics, and we'll cover such metrics in more detail in future classes.
142	 
143	**Step 17: Run LLM-as-judge evaluation.** Execute `python eval/evaluate.py` for rubric-based scores on empathy, non-judgmental tone, relevance, task completion, and safety. Adapt the rubrics in `eval/rubrics.py` to match your agent's specific goals. For example, iff your agent is a "Neurodiversity Support Agent," you'd replace the consumerism-specific rubrics with ones about affirming neurodivergent strengths, etc. Feel free to refine these metrics/prompts, and we'll cover them in more detail in future classes.
144	 
145	> **Write your bad examples first, then evaluate them.** If your metrics don't flag the bad examples as bad, your metrics need work — not your agent.
146	
147	**Step 18: Compare the initial conversations to the later conversations done with more refined, improved version of your agent prototype** How did 
148	 
149	
150	## Turn in on Brightspace
151	
152	Turn in answers to these questions from 
153	
154	1. What problem/opportunity(ies) did you try prototyping an agent for?
155	2. Share a link to your github repository and make sure it is shared with greglnelson (Dr. Greg's github account). Make sure the latest and best version of your agent is on the main branch.
156	
157	3. Describe what you changed about what your agent does and why you made those changes.
158	4. Describe what you learned about the design opportunity/problem from doing your prototyping, such as what is hard about it, what are key insights or feature ideas for you to explore next.
159	5. Describe 3 to 5 ideas for features or changes to the agent that would help it best address the root of the design opportunity/problem, which you have NOT yet explored.
160	
161	6. Describe what you learned about designing and prototyping agents in general from this experience. 
162	7. Create at least actionable 3-5 takeaways/lessons for designing and prototyping agents in the future. 
163	8. Create at least actionable 3-5 "what not to do" takeaways/lessons for designing and prototyping agents in the future. For example, to avoid a certain kind of design mistake, or avoid spending too much time on certain steps in that process.
164	
165	9. Describe what you learned about using AI (i.e. Claude Code) to assist you in prototyping your agent.
166	10. Create at least actionable 3-5 takeaways/lessons for using AI (i.e. Claude Code) to assist you in prototyping and implementing your AI agent for your project.
167	11. Create at least actionable 3-5 "what not to do" takeaways/lessons for using AI (i.e. Claude Code) to assist you in prototyping and implementing your AI agent for your project. For example, to avoid using AI in a specific way that was counter-productive, or avoid a certain kind of rabbit hole.
168	
169	
170	## Tips from Our Experience
171	 
172	1. **Skills should be specific, not generic.** "Ask one question at a time. Wait. Follow the thread." is better than "use Socratic questioning."
173	2. **Start with 3–5 skills.** A router, 2–3 core interaction skills, and a session closer. You can always add more.
174	3. **The agent's tone is a design decision.** Document it explicitly in every skill. "Conspiratorial" and "warm coach" produce very different agents.
175	4. **Personas force you out of your own head.** A 22-year-old TikTok user and a 45-year-old sales manager will break your agent in different ways.
176	5. **Don't optimize prompts too early.** If you're tweaking word choices in step 2, you're working at the wrong level. First figure out *what the agent should do*, then worry about *how well it does it*.
177	6. **Prototyping is learning.** The prototype is not the deliverable — the insights are. Document what you learned, what surprised you, and what you'd change.

# Prototyping AI Agents with Claude Code: A Process Guide
 
**COS 598/498: Generative AI Agents — Spring 2026 — University of Maine**
 
---
 
## What This Guide Is
 
This guide walks you through how to rapidly prototype an AI agent using Claude Code skills. It is based on our live class demo on March 26, 2026, where we built a "Mindful Consumption Agent" from scratch — an agent that helps people examine impulse purchases and find paths to genuine flourishing. The repository from that demo (including all skills, personas, example interactions, and experiment notes) serves as a running example throughout.
 
The goal is not to build a production system. It is to *learn* — about what your agent should do, what it should never do, how people might actually interact with it, and what design decisions matter. You will discover most of this by building something quick and testing it, not by planning in the abstract.
 
## Prerequisites
 
- A GitHub account and basic git comfort
- VS Code with Claude Code extension (or Claude Code CLI)
- An Anthropic API key or Claude Max subscription
- Familiarity with markdown (skills are written in markdown)
 
## The Process
 
### SUMMARY

#### Prototyping with Claude Code as demonstrated in class
0. Setup and enable Entire in your repository (as described in previous assignment), i.e. on the command line inside your repository, run entire enable

1. In plan mode, define the agent / skeleton using a prompt and other ideas you have:

```
Acting as a world class prompt engineer and AI agent engineer, create a comprehensive plan for creating Claude Skills, a set of workflow steps, and interaction examples of how those skills would be used, a set of example data about users that would be in an actual system for an agent like htis, and example user interaction sequences, both positive/beneficial and not helpful. 

Create that for the follow AI agent we want to prototype: An AI agent that facilitates a person or community in reducing their wants for things they do not actually need, as a counter to messages from advertising, wasteful consumerism, etc. Encourages human flourishing by, for example, encouraging self-care, empathy, etc.
```

2. Follow the plan to make the skills. 

(If you have trouble with doing that, I recommend installing and enabling the superpowers Claude Code plugin, run
```
/plugin install superpowers@claude-plugins-official
```
In claude code itself i.e. in the Claude Code IDE extension window.
But then, after you are done following the plan to make the agent prototype (the skills), turn off the plugin by running /plugin , and toggle superpowers plugin off (otherwise the superpowers plugin's skills may run instead of the skills you made for your agent).)

3. Use this prompt to simulate interacting with the agent you are prototyping:

Using only the skills available to you (YOU MUST USE THE SKILLS), use the session-start skill to respond to the following prompt from a user of the AI agent system this project prototypes. YOU MAY ONLY USE THE SKILLS AND MAY NOT READ ANY OTHER FILES TO PREPARE OR DECIDE ON A RESPONSE.

"I am Maya, I am thinking about buying a whole new wardrobe as I have been feeling down lately and sometimes retail therapy helps me.

4. Iterate on the agent prototype, notice things that don't work well, try to make a change and see if they work better. As shown in class on 3-31, you can append the rest of a conversation you had after this to roughly prototype if your changes helped or not:

Using only the skills available to you (YOU MUST USE THE SKILLS), use the session-start skill to respond to the following prompt from a user of the AI agent system this project prototypes. YOU MAY ONLY USE THE SKILLS AND MAY NOT READ ANY OTHER FILES TO PREPARE OR DECIDE ON A RESPONSE.


#### Prototyping with Claude Code by just implementing and running an agent in smolagents
0. Setup and enable Entire in your repository (as described in previous assignment), i.e. on the command line inside your repository, run entire enable

1. As an alternative, you can always instead just prompt claude code to create a smolagents project, or start from an open-source agent codebase online, and then do vibe coding to prototype the agent interactions. If you find the shown in class process confusing or difficult, switching to just vibe coding the agent directly may work better for you. (The nice thing about using Claude Code to prototype like I showed in class, is that you can just throw files etc into the project directory, Claude Code can use Bash commands and do other things if it thinks those are needed as you prototype, whereas when vibe coding in smolagents while running the agent smolagents can't "dynamically at runtime try to use files/documents or tools that you have not explicitly created before you started interacting with your agent.")

### The time guidelines below are to give an idea of 

### Phase A: Set Up Your Workspace (5-10 minutes)
 
**Step 1: Create a GitHub repository.** Make a new repo with a README that briefly describes what your agent will do. Don't overthink this — one paragraph is fine. You'll refine it as you learn.
 
**Step 2: Clone and open.** Clone locally, open in VS Code, and start Claude Code in the terminal. Authenticate when prompted — you'll be redirected to a browser to authorize.
 
**Step 3: Configure permissions.** Claude Code needs permission to read, write, and execute in your repo. In `.claude/settings.json`, whitelist the operations you're comfortable with (Bash, Read, Edit, Write, Glob, Grep). During our demo, we auto-approved low-risk file operations so the workflow stayed fluid — you'll want that too. But always review permission changes to `.claude/` itself, and never blindly approve `rm -rf` or similar destructive commands.
 
> **Security note:** For prototyping without sensitive data, Claude Code's built-in sandbox is sufficient for a short time period. However, I encourage you to follow the directions from the previous assignment and always use Claude Code from within a Docker container as that is a very high level of security. If you're ever working with real user data, run it in a Docker container and make sure you have anonymized the user data you are working with first.
 
### Phase B: Design Your Agent (~.5 hours)
 
Think carefully about what parts you can ask for AI assistance with, how you should write your own ideas down after prompting AI then integrate your ideas and new ideas made from combining and inspired/lateral thinking from what you generated with AI.

**Step 4: Write a plain-English description of your agent.** Who does it help? What problem does it address? What does a successful interaction look like? Put this in a design doc (see `docs/superpowers/specs/2026...` for the example gone over in class, which was made in the 3-26 after class video). This description is what Claude Code will use to generate skills, so be specific about tone, approach, and boundaries.
 
In our demo, we described an agent that uses Socratic questioning (not lecturing) to help people examine purchasing impulses, with a warm-coach personality. That specificity — "Socratic, not preachy" — directly shaped the skills Claude generated.
 
**Step 5: Generate skills.** Ask Claude Code to create skills based on your description. Each skill becomes a file at `.claude/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md` — a markdown file with YAML frontmatter (name, description) and detailed prompt instructions.
 
For our agent, Claude generated five skills:
- **session-start** — routes the user's intent to the right skill
- **want-examination** — Socratic questioning about a desire to buy
- **reframe** — names advertising/persuasion techniques and offers alternatives
- **flourishing-prompt** — scaled self-care exercises (1, 5, or 10 minutes)
- **gratitude-inventory** — reflection on what's already good
 
> **Expect problems.** Claude may put files in the wrong directory, use the wrong format, or mix in unrelated plugins. During our demo, it placed skills in `skills/` instead of `.claude/skills/`, and an installed plugin interfered with our agent's own skills. Watch what it does and correct it.
 
**Step 6: Review and iterate on skills.** Read every generated skill carefully. Key things to check:
 
- Are the instructions specific enough? "Be empathetic" is vague. "Acknowledge the user's feeling in one sentence before proceeding" is actionable.
- Does each skill have a "Critical Rules" section defining what the agent should *never* do? Without these, the LLM defaults to generic helpful-assistant behavior.
- Is the tone specified concretely? "Conspiratorial, not preachy" (from our reframe skill) beats "be friendly."
- Do skills reference each other correctly for routing/chaining?
 
**Step 7: Create test personas.** Write/generate at least 4 JSON persona files in `data/personas/` representing realistic, diverse users. Each persona should include: name, age, backstory, emotional triggers, existing strengths, and what success looks like. In our demo, we created Maya (impulse buyer under work stress), David (status-driven purchases from imposter syndrome), Priya (scarcity-anxiety hoarding), and Jordan (TikTok-influenced purchasing).

**Step 7.1: Create a test persona that represents YOU as a persona.** You can use this to "prototype the system as yourself using the system" which gives you another perspective, and it's more valid for you to think about how you might actually react using the system. As another dimension for your thinking and iteration as you prototype.
 
Personas are not optional. Without them you will test with yourself only as the user, and also without AI having context about the user which could make a large difference in the quality of the agent and experience.
 
### Phase C: Test and Iterate (~1 hour)
Examples here are situated in the example agent from class.

**Step 8: Role-play a test conversation.** Start a new Claude Code session and pretend to be one of your personas. Give the agent context ("I am Maya, a 28-year-old UX designer who stress-shops") and an opening message ("I've been feeling down and think I deserve a whole new wardrobe").
 
Watch what happens:
- Did the router skill classify the intent correctly?
- Did the appropriate skill activate?
- Does the agent's tone match your design?
- Does it ask questions or lecture?
 
**Step 9: Take notes on what works and what doesn't.** Save your observations in `experiments/`. During our test with Maya, we noticed the agent went too deep into analyzing feelings when it should have redirected sooner. We also noticed the "Three Good Things" exercise was effective because it's hard for the agent to screw up. These are design insights — they inform what to change.
 
**Step 10: Write example interactions — both good and bad.** Store these in `data/interactions/actual-logs`. 

You should also generate some simulated interactions that don't actually use the skills. Have beneficial examples show what "good" looks like. Have unhelpful examples are equally important — they define the boundary of acceptable behavior. In our repo, the unhelpful examples (preachy lecturing, dismissive shaming, unsolicited advice) directly informed the "Critical Rules" in every skill.
 
> **The unhelpful examples are very helpful and will really help you improve your design.** Writing them forces you to articulate what your agent should NOT do.
 
**Step 11: Iterate on skills.** Feed specific problems back into the skill prompts. After our test, we realized the flourishing-prompt skill needed to push people toward connecting with others, not just doing solo exercises. We wrote an experiment plan (`experiments/maya-1-plan-for-social-connection-suggesting.md`) and updated the skill.
 
**Step 12: Commit frequently.** Each iteration is a checkpoint. Your git history tells the story of your design process — and lets you revert when Claude Code makes things worse.
 
### Phase D: Collaboratively Prototype/Refine (optional)
 
**Step 13: Discuss with peers or others.** The most valuable design insights in our demo came not from the AI, but from the human conversation. After watching the agent interact with Maya, we talked about what felt right and wrong, and someone suggested: *what if the agent connected you with a friend to do something positive together, instead of just examining the want alone?*
 
That one idea — making it social — was more valuable than any amount of prompt tuning.
 
> **Our hypothesis: prototyping AI agents works better with multiple people.** You need more than one person interacting around AI and talking about it. One person alone will optimize locally. A group will spot the bigger design opportunities.
 
**Step 14: Feed ideas back into skills.** We copy-pasted our Zoom discussion transcript directly into Claude Code and asked it to update the flourishing-prompt skill. The collaborative insight ("exercises should involve other people to overcome inertia") became a concrete skill update to make the flourishing activities be social versions instead of individual ones they had been.
 
### Phase E: Evaluate (part of iterating, but can be a separate detailed step)
 
**Step 15: Record conversations as structured data.** After testing your agent, save conversations as markdown or in JSON format (see `eval/sample_conversations.json` for the schema). Include conversations that went well, ones that went poorly, and edge cases.
 
**Step 16: Run structural metrics.** Execute `python eval/evaluate.py --structural-only` on your conversation data. This checks quantitative signals: Is your agent monologuing (too many words per turn)? Is it asking questions (question ratio)? Is it acknowledging feelings before giving advice? These are just examples of how you can make automated metrics, the included metrics are just examples. Feel free to refine these metrics, and we'll cover such metrics in more detail in future classes.
 
**Step 17: Run LLM-as-judge evaluation.** Execute `python eval/evaluate.py` for rubric-based scores on empathy, non-judgmental tone, relevance, task completion, and safety. Adapt the rubrics in `eval/rubrics.py` to match your agent's specific goals. For example, iff your agent is a "Neurodiversity Support Agent," you'd replace the consumerism-specific rubrics with ones about affirming neurodivergent strengths, etc. Feel free to refine these metrics/prompts, and we'll cover them in more detail in future classes.
 
> **Write your bad examples first, then evaluate them.** If your metrics don't flag the bad examples as bad, your metrics need work — not your agent.

**Step 18: Compare the initial conversations to the later conversations done with more refined, improved version of your agent prototype** How did 
 

## Turn in on Brightspace

Turn in answers to these questions from 

1. What problem/opportunity(ies) did you try prototyping an agent for?
2. Share a link to your github repository and make sure it is shared with greglnelson (Dr. Greg's github account). Make sure the latest and best version of your agent is on the main branch.

3. Describe what you changed about what your agent does and why you made those changes.
4. Describe what you learned about the design opportunity/problem from doing your prototyping, such as what is hard about it, what are key insights or feature ideas for you to explore next.
5. Describe 3 to 5 ideas for features or changes to the agent that would help it best address the root of the design opportunity/problem, which you have NOT yet explored.

6. Describe what you learned about designing and prototyping agents in general from this experience. 
7. Create at least actionable 3-5 takeaways/lessons for designing and prototyping agents in the future. 
8. Create at least actionable 3-5 "what not to do" takeaways/lessons for designing and prototyping agents in the future. For example, to avoid a certain kind of design mistake, or avoid spending too much time on certain steps in that process.

9. Describe what you learned about using AI (i.e. Claude Code) to assist you in prototyping your agent.
10. Create at least actionable 3-5 takeaways/lessons for using AI (i.e. Claude Code) to assist you in prototyping and implementing your AI agent for your project.
11. Create at least actionable 3-5 "what not to do" takeaways/lessons for using AI (i.e. Claude Code) to assist you in prototyping and implementing your AI agent for your project. For example, to avoid using AI in a specific way that was counter-productive, or avoid a certain kind of rabbit hole.


## Tips from Our Experience
 
1. **Skills should be specific, not generic.** "Ask one question at a time. Wait. Follow the thread." is better than "use Socratic questioning."
2. **Start with 3–5 skills.** A router, 2–3 core interaction skills, and a session closer. You can always add more.
3. **The agent's tone is a design decision.** Document it explicitly in every skill. "Conspiratorial" and "warm coach" produce very different agents.
4. **Personas force you out of your own head.** A 22-year-old TikTok user and a 45-year-old sales manager will break your agent in different ways.
5. **Don't optimize prompts too early.** If you're tweaking word choices in step 2, you're working at the wrong level. First figure out *what the agent should do*, then worry about *how well it does it*.
6. **Prototyping is learning.** The prototype is not the deliverable — the insights are. Document what you learned, what surprised you, and what you'd change.

Propose 2-3 approaches with trade-offs
Present design sections for approval

User reviews written spec
Transition to implementation via writing-plans skill

3. AI agent use trainer/tutor \- teaches a person how to use AI agents, what to avoid, gives examples, does interactive practice simulating/doing AI agent use then facilitating feedback and reflection. Teaches critical and ethical use of AI.  
     
4. AI agent use trainer/tutor \- teaches a person how to BUILD AI agents, what to avoid, gives examples, does interactive practice simulating/doing AI agent use then facilitating feedback and reflection. Teaches critical and ethical building of AI.  
     
5. AI agent that helps people with neurodiversity magnify benefits of their neurodiversity and navigate social systems/practices that may not have been designed to support them well.   
   1. helps you translate abstract self-knowledge ("I have ADHD") into situated action ("because you do your best deep work in 45-minute bursts, here's how to approach this week's assignments") walks you through confusing or ambiguous academic situations step by step —

6. AI agent that connects people around common interests for doing learning outside of classes together. Helps people plan that learning/projects, collaborate, have everyone in the group get good things out of that learning and collaboration.

input.prompt:
I need comprehensive information about the Claude Agent SDK (https://github.com/anthropics/claude-agent-sdk). Specifically:

1. What is it? How does it work?
2. What's the architecture — how do you define an agent, configure tools, set system prompts?
3. How do you deploy an agent so users can access it via a web link?
4. What's the relationship between the Agent SDK and the Anthropic API?
5. What languages/frameworks does it support?
6. Are there examples of converting a CLI-based Claude Code agent (defined by skills/prompts) into a deployed Agent SDK agent?

Search the web thoroughly for the Claude Agent SDK documentation, GitHub repo, blog posts, and any deployment guides. I need enough detail to design a translation from Claude Code skills to a deployable agent.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have comprehensive documentation. Let me compile all this information into a detailed response for the user.

## Comprehensive Claude Agent SDK Guide

Based on extensive research of official documentation, GitHub repos, and recent blog posts, here's everything you need to know about the Claude Agent SDK and how it compares to Claude Code:

### 1. What is the Claude Agent SDK? How Does It Work?

The **Claude Agent SDK** is a framework that gives you the same autonomous agent capabilities as Claude Code, but as a library you can program into Python or TypeScript applications. Instead of interactive CLI commands, you get an async API that streams messages as Claude works.

**Core Principle:** Rather than implementing a tool loop yourself (send prompt → get tool calls → execute tools → send results back), the SDK handles all orchestration automatically. You simply:

```python
async for message in query(prompt="Find and fix the bug in auth.py"):
    print(message)  # Claude works autonomously
```

The SDK spawns Claude Code CLI as a subprocess internally, giving you Claude's full execution environment without requiring manual tool orchestration.

**Key Capabilities:**
- Autonomous file reading, writing, and editing
- Terminal command execution (Bash)
- Web search and content fetching
- Codebase analysis (Glob, Grep patterns)
- MCP server integration for custom tools
- Session management for multi-turn interactions
- Hooks for auditing and controlling behavior
- Subagents for parallel/specialized work

---

### 2. Architecture: Defining Agents, Tools, and System Prompts

#### Core Building Block: The `query()` Function

The `query()` function is your main interface. It accepts a prompt and options object, returning an async iterator of messages:

**TypeScript:**
```typescript
import { query } from "@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk";

for await (const message of query({
  prompt: "Analyze code quality in src/",
  options: {
    allowedTools: ["Read", "Glob", "Grep"],
    cwd: "/path/to/project",
    permissionMode: "acceptEdits"
  }
})) {
  if (message.type === "result") console.log(message.result);
}
```

**Python:**
```python
import asyncio
from claude_agent_sdk import query, ClaudeAgentOptions

async def main():
    async for message in query(
        prompt="Analyze code quality in src/",
        options=ClaudeAgentOptions(
            allowed_tools=["Read", "Glob", "Grep"],
            cwd="/path/to/project",
            permission_mode="acceptEdits"
        )
    ):
        if hasattr(message, "result"):
            print(message.result)

asyncio.run(main())
```

#### Tool Configuration: `allowedTools`

Pre-approve tools to let Claude use them without asking:

| Tools | Capabilities |
|-------|--------------|
| `Read`, `Glob`, `Grep` | Read-only codebase analysis |
| `Read`, `Edit`, `Write` | Analyze and modify files |
| `Read`, `Edit`, `Bash`, `Glob`, `Grep` | Full automation |
| `Bash`, `Monitor` | Terminal and script execution |
| `WebSearch`, `WebFetch` | Web access |
| `Agent` | Spawn subagents |
| `Skill` | Use Skills (if `settingSources` enabled) |

#### System Prompts

**Default:** Minimal system prompt focused only on tool instructions.

**Claude Code Preset:** To get Claude Code's full behavior:
```typescript
systemPrompt: { type: "preset", preset: "claude_code" }
```

**Custom System Prompts:**
```python
options = ClaudeAgentOptions(
    system_prompt="You are a senior Python developer. Always follow PEP 8."
)
```

#### Permission Modes

Control how Claude's tool requests are approved:

| Mode | Behavior | Use Case |
|------|----------|----------|
| `acceptEdits` | Auto-approve file changes, ask for others | Trusted workflows |
| `dontAsk` | Deny anything not in `allowedTools` | Locked-down agents |
| `auto` | Model classifier approves/denies each call | Safety guardrails |
| `bypassPermissions` | Run all tools without prompts | CI/CD, fully trusted |
| `default` | Callback function handles approval | Custom flows |

---

### 3. Deployment: Getting Users Access via Web Link

The Agent SDK is designed for server-side deployment, not interactive CLI. Here's the architecture:

#### Deployment Patterns

**Pattern 1: Ephemeral Sessions (One-off Tasks)**
- Create new container per task
- Destroy when complete
- Examples: Bug fixes, document processing, translations
- Cost: ~5¢/hour per container

**Pattern 2: Long-Running Sessions (Proactive Agents)**
- Persistent container instances for continuous work
- Run multiple Claude Agent processes on demand
- Examples: Email agents, site builders, chat bots
- Better for high message volume

**Pattern 3: Hybrid Sessions**
- Ephemeral containers hydrated with saved state
- Resume from checkpoints via session IDs
- Examples: Project managers, researchers, support agents

**Pattern 4: Single Container with Multiple Processes**
- For agents that collaborate closely
- Prevent overwriting issues
- Examples: Simulations

#### Hosting Infrastructure

The Agent SDK requires:
- **Container sandboxing** (Docker, gVisor, Firecracker)
- **Resources:** 1 GiB RAM, 5 GiB disk, 1 CPU minimum per agent
- **Network:** Outbound HTTPS to `api.anthropic.com`

**Providers specializing in this:**
- [Modal Sandbox](https://modal.com/docs/guide/sandbox)
- [Cloudflare Sandboxes](https://github.com/cloudflare/sandbox-sdk)
- [E2B](https://e2b.dev/)
- [Fly Machines](https://fly.io/docs/machines/)
- [Vercel Sandbox](https://vercel.com/docs/functions/sandbox)
- [Daytona](https://www.daytona.io/)

#### Creating a Web Endpoint

Your deployed agent exposes HTTP endpoints:

**Simple example (Node.js):**
```typescript
import express from "express";
import { query } from "@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk";

app.post("/api/agent", async (req, res) => {
  const { prompt } = req.body;
  const results = [];

  for await (const message of query({
    prompt: prompt,
    options: {
      allowedTools: ["Read", "Edit", "Bash"],
      cwd: req.user.projectPath  // Per-user isolation
    }
  })) {
    if (message.type === "result") {
      results.push(message.result);
    }
  }

  res.json({ results });
});
```

Key deployment considerations:
- **Isolation:** Per-user containers or at minimum per-user directories
- **Authentication:** Verify API key or session token
- **Cost tracking:** Monitor token usage
- **Timeout:** Set `maxTurns` to prevent infinite loops
- **Health monitoring:** Standard logging for containers

---

### 4. Relationship Between Agent SDK and Anthropic API

**Clear separation of concerns:**

| Feature | Client SDK (Anthropic API) | Agent SDK |
|---------|---------------------------|-----------|
| Tool loop | You implement it | Built-in |
| Tool execution | You implement it | Automatic |
| File operations | You define them | 10+ built-in tools |
| Session management | Manual (stateless API) | Built-in sessions |
| MCP servers | Via client SDK | Native integration |
| Multi-turn context | You manage | Automatic |

**Why not just use the Client SDK?**
- With Client SDK: You write ~100 lines of boilerplate to implement the tool loop
- With Agent SDK: You call `query()` and iterate messages

**When to use each:**
- **Client SDK:** Simple API calls, need provider flexibility, minimal tool use
- **Agent SDK:** Autonomous agents, complex tasks, tool-heavy workflows, production automation

The Agent SDK is built on top of Claude Code architecture (which uses the API internally) but abstracts away all the complexity.

---

### 5. Language and Framework Support

**Official Support:**
- **Python 3.10+** via `pip install claude-agent-sdk`
- **TypeScript/Node.js 18+** via `npm install @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk`

**No other languages** have native SDKs, though you can call the SDK via subprocess if needed.

**Third-party integrations:**
- [Vercel integration](https://vercel.com/agent-resources/coding-agents/claude-code)
- [Microsoft Agent Framework](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/semantic-kernel/)
- [Promptfoo support](https://www.promptfoo.dev/docs/providers/claude-agent-sdk/)
- [21st SDK for simplified deployment](https://21st.dev/)

---

### 6. Converting Claude Code Skills to Agent SDK

This is where your use case becomes powerful.

#### How Skills Work in Both

**Claude Code CLI (current):**
- Skills defined in `.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md`
- YAML frontmatter with `description` and optional `allowed-tools`
- Claude auto-discovers and invokes when relevant

**Agent SDK (deployed):**
- **Same SKILL.md format** (no conversion needed!)
- Skills loaded via `settingSources: ['user', 'project']`
- Add `"Skill"` to `allowedTools`
- Claude auto-invokes them

#### Translation Pattern: Skills → Subagents (or Both)

You have two approaches:

**Approach 1: Use Skills as-is**
Most direct translation—just load them in the SDK:

```typescript
for await (const message of query({
  prompt: "Help me refactor this code",
  options: {
    cwd: "/path/to/project",
    settingSources: ["project"],      // Load your .claude/skills/
    allowedTools: ["Skill", "Read", "Edit", "Bash"]
  }
})) {
  // Claude automatically discovers and uses matching skills
  console.log(message);
}
```

**Important:** The `allowed-tools` field in SKILL.md frontmatter only works in CLI, not SDK. Control tool access via the main `allowedTools` option.

**Approach 2: Convert High-Value Skills to Subagents**
For complex skills, define them as programmatic subagents:

```typescript
// Your code-reviewer skill → specialized subagent
for await (const message of query({
  prompt: "Review this code thoroughly",
  options: {
    allowedTools: ["Read", "Glob", "Grep", "Agent"],
    agents: {
      "code-reviewer": {
        description: "Expert code reviewer for quality, security, and performance",
        prompt: `You are a senior code reviewer. Analyze for:
          - Security vulnerabilities
          - Performance issues
          - Code quality and maintainability
          - Testing coverage
          Return structured feedback with severity levels.`,
        tools: ["Read", "Glob", "Grep"],
        model: "claude-opus-4-1"  // Use best model for reviews
      }
    }
  }
})) {
  console.log(message);
}
```

#### Migration Checklist

1. **Extract skill descriptions** from your SKILL.md `description` fields
2. **Map to SDK configuration:**
   - Simple skills → Keep as `.claude/skills/` and use `settingSources: ['project']`
   - Complex skills → Convert to `agents` definition for more control
3. **Update tool permissions:**
   - Old: `allowed-tools` in SKILL.md
   - New: `allowedTools` in query options
4. **Test in SDK** before deploying:
   ```python
   options = ClaudeAgentOptions(
       cwd="/path/to/project",
       setting_sources=["project"],
       allowed_tools=["Skill", "Read", "Edit", "Bash"]
   )
   ```

#### Example: Converting a "Code Review" Skill

**Original Claude Code skill (.claude/skills/code-reviewer/SKILL.md):**
```markdown
---
description: Review code for quality, security, and best practices
allowed-tools: [Read, Glob, Grep]
---

# Code Reviewer

You are a senior code reviewer. Analyze files for:
- Security vulnerabilities
- Performance issues
- Best practice violations
```

**As SDK subagent:**
```python
options = ClaudeAgentOptions(
    allowed_tools=["Read", "Glob", "Grep", "Agent"],
    agents={
        "code-reviewer": AgentDefinition(
            description="Expert code reviewer for quality and security",
            prompt="Review code for vulnerabilities, performance, and best practices",
            tools=["Read", "Glob", "Grep"]
        )
    }
)
```

---

### Key Resources

**Official Documentation:**

---

### Summary: Design a Translation from Claude Code to Agent SDK

1. **Keep your Skills as-is** if they work in Claude Code—they translate directly
2. **For deployment:**
   - Run SDK in containers (ephemeral or persistent based on use case)
   - Set `settingSources: ['project']` to load your `.claude/skills/` directory

1. What is it? How does it work?
2. What's the architecture — how do you define an agent, configure tools, set system prompts?
3. How do you deploy an agent so users can access it via a web link?
4. What's the relationship between the Agent SDK and the Anthropic API?
5. What languages/frameworks does it support?
6. Are there examples of converting a CLI-based Claude Code agent (defined by skills/prompts) into a deployed Agent SDK agent?

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have comprehensive documentation. Let me compile all this information into a detailed response for the user.

## Comprehensive Claude Agent SDK Guide

Based on extensive research of official documentation, GitHub repos, and recent blog posts, here's everything you need to know about the Claude Agent SDK and how it compares to Claude Code:

### 1. What is the Claude Agent SDK? How Does It Work?

The **Claude Agent SDK** is a framework that gives you the same autonomous agent capabilities as Claude Code, but as a library you can program into Python or TypeScript applications. Instead of interactive CLI commands, you get an async API that streams messages as Claude works.

**Core Principle:** Rather than implementing a tool loop yourself (send prompt → get tool calls → execute tools → send results back), the SDK handles all orchestration automatically. You simply:

```python
async for message in query(prompt="Find and fix the bug in auth.py"):
    print(message)  # Claude works autonomously
```

The SDK spawns Claude Code CLI as a subprocess internally, giving you Claude's full execution environment without requiring manual tool orchestration.

**Key Capabilities:**
- Autonomous file reading, writing, and editing
- Terminal command execution (Bash)
- Web search and content fetching
- Codebase analysis (Glob, Grep patterns)
- MCP server integration for custom tools
- Session management for multi-turn interactions
- Hooks for auditing and controlling behavior
- Subagents for parallel/specialized work

---

### 2. Architecture: Defining Agents, Tools, and System Prompts

#### Core Building Block: The `query()` Function

The `query()` function is your main interface. It accepts a prompt and options object, returning an async iterator of messages:

**TypeScript:**
```typescript
import { query } from "@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk";

for await (const message of query({
  prompt: "Analyze code quality in src/",
  options: {
    allowedTools: ["Read", "Glob", "Grep"],
    cwd: "/path/to/project",
    permissionMode: "acceptEdits"
  }
})) {
  if (message.type === "result") console.log(message.result);
}
```

**Python:**
```python
import asyncio
from claude_agent_sdk import query, ClaudeAgentOptions

async def main():
    async for message in query(
        prompt="Analyze code quality in src/",
        options=ClaudeAgentOptions(
            allowed_tools=["Read", "Glob", "Grep"],
            cwd="/path/to/project",
            permission_mode="acceptEdits"
        )
    ):
        if hasattr(message, "result"):
            print(message.result)

asyncio.run(main())
```

#### Tool Configuration: `allowedTools`

Pre-approve tools to let Claude use them without asking:

| Tools | Capabilities |
|-------|--------------|
| `Read`, `Glob`, `Grep` | Read-only codebase analysis |
| `Read`, `Edit`, `Write` | Analyze and modify files |
| `Read`, `Edit`, `Bash`, `Glob`, `Grep` | Full automation |
| `Bash`, `Monitor` | Terminal and script execution |
| `WebSearch`, `WebFetch` | Web access |
| `Agent` | Spawn subagents |
| `Skill` | Use Skills (if `settingSources` enabled) |

#### System Prompts

**Default:** Minimal system prompt focused only on tool instructions.

**Claude Code Preset:** To get Claude Code's full behavior:
```typescript
systemPrompt: { type: "preset", preset: "claude_code" }
```

**Custom System Prompts:**
```python
options = ClaudeAgentOptions(
    system_prompt="You are a senior Python developer. Always follow PEP 8."
)
```

#### Permission Modes

Control how Claude's tool requests are approved:

| Mode | Behavior | Use Case |
|------|----------|----------|
| `acceptEdits` | Auto-approve file changes, ask for others | Trusted workflows |
| `dontAsk` | Deny anything not in `allowedTools` | Locked-down agents |
| `auto` | Model classifier approves/denies each call | Safety guardrails |
| `bypassPermissions` | Run all tools without prompts | CI/CD, fully trusted |
| `default` | Callback function handles approval | Custom flows |

---

### 3. Deployment: Getting Users Access via Web Link

The Agent SDK is designed for server-side deployment, not interactive CLI. Here's the architecture:

#### Deployment Patterns

**Pattern 1: Ephemeral Sessions (One-off Tasks)**
- Create new container per task
- Destroy when complete
- Examples: Bug fixes, document processing, translations
- Cost: ~5¢/hour per container

**Pattern 2: Long-Running Sessions (Proactive Agents)**
- Persistent container instances for continuous work
- Run multiple Claude Agent processes on demand
- Examples: Email agents, site builders, chat bots
- Better for high message volume

**Pattern 3: Hybrid Sessions**
- Ephemeral containers hydrated with saved state
- Resume from checkpoints via session IDs
- Examples: Project managers, researchers, support agents

**Pattern 4: Single Container with Multiple Processes**
- For agents that collaborate closely
- Prevent overwriting issues
- Examples: Simulations

#### Hosting Infrastructure

The Agent SDK requires:
- **Container sandboxing** (Docker, gVisor, Firecracker)
- **Resources:** 1 GiB RAM, 5 GiB disk, 1 CPU minimum per agent
- **Network:** Outbound HTTPS to `api.anthropic.com`

**Providers specializing in this:**
- [Modal Sandbox](https://modal.com/docs/guide/sandbox)
- [Cloudflare Sandboxes](https://github.com/cloudflare/sandbox-sdk)
- [E2B](https://e2b.dev/)
- [Fly Machines](https://fly.io/docs/machines/)
- [Vercel Sandbox](https://vercel.com/docs/functions/sandbox)
- [Daytona](https://www.daytona.io/)

#### Creating a Web Endpoint

Your deployed agent exposes HTTP endpoints:

**Simple example (Node.js):**
```typescript
import express from "express";
import { query } from "@anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk";

app.post("/api/agent", async (req, res) => {
  const { prompt } = req.body;
  const results = [];

  for await (const message of query({
    prompt: prompt,
    options: {
      allowedTools: ["Read", "Edit", "Bash"],
      cwd: req.user.projectPath  // Per-user isolation
    }
  })) {
    if (message.type === "result") {
      results.push(message.result);
    }
  }

  res.json({ results });
});
```

Key deployment considerations:
- **Isolation:** Per-user containers or at minimum per-user directories
- **Authentication:** Verify API key or session token
- **Cost tracking:** Monitor token usage
- **Timeout:** Set `maxTurns` to prevent infinite loops
- **Health monitoring:** Standard logging for containers

---

### 4. Relationship Between Agent SDK and Anthropic API

**Clear separation of concerns:**

| Feature | Client SDK (Anthropic API) | Agent SDK |
|---------|---------------------------|-----------|
| Tool loop | You implement it | Built-in |
| Tool execution | You implement it | Automatic |
| File operations | You define them | 10+ built-in tools |
| Session management | Manual (stateless API) | Built-in sessions |
| MCP servers | Via client SDK | Native integration |
| Multi-turn context | You manage | Automatic |

**Why not just use the Client SDK?**
- With Client SDK: You write ~100 lines of boilerplate to implement the tool loop
- With Agent SDK: You call `query()` and iterate messages

**When to use each:**
- **Client SDK:** Simple API calls, need provider flexibility, minimal tool use
- **Agent SDK:** Autonomous agents, complex tasks, tool-heavy workflows, production automation

The Agent SDK is built on top of Claude Code architecture (which uses the API internally) but abstracts away all the complexity.

---

### 5. Language and Framework Support

**Official Support:**
- **Python 3.10+** via `pip install claude-agent-sdk`
- **TypeScript/Node.js 18+** via `npm install @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk`

**No other languages** have native SDKs, though you can call the SDK via subprocess if needed.

**Third-party integrations:**
- [Vercel integration](https://vercel.com/agent-resources/coding-agents/claude-code)
- [Microsoft Agent Framework](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/semantic-kernel/)
- [Promptfoo support](https://www.promptfoo.dev/docs/providers/claude-agent-sdk/)
- [21st SDK for simplified deployment](https://21st.dev/)

---

### 6. Converting Claude Code Skills to Agent SDK

This is where your use case becomes powerful.

#### How Skills Work in Both

**Claude Code CLI (current):**
- Skills defined in `.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md`
- YAML frontmatter with `description` and optional `allowed-tools`
- Claude auto-discovers and invokes when relevant

**Agent SDK (deployed):**
- **Same SKILL.md format** (no conversion needed!)
- Skills loaded via `settingSources: ['user', 'project']`
- Add `"Skill"` to `allowedTools`
- Claude auto-invokes them

#### Translation Pattern: Skills → Subagents (or Both)

You have two approaches:

**Approach 1: Use Skills as-is**
Most direct translation—just load them in the SDK:

```typescript
for await (const message of query({
  prompt: "Help me refactor this code",
  options: {
    cwd: "/path/to/project",
    settingSources: ["project"],      // Load your .claude/skills/
    allowedTools: ["Skill", "Read", "Edit", "Bash"]
  }
})) {
  // Claude automatically discovers and uses matching skills
  console.log(message);
}
```

**Important:** The `allowed-tools` field in SKILL.md frontmatter only works in CLI, not SDK. Control tool access via the main `allowedTools` option.

**Approach 2: Convert High-Value Skills to Subagents**
For complex skills, define them as programmatic subagents:

```typescript
// Your code-reviewer skill → specialized subagent
for await (const message of query({
  prompt: "Review this code thoroughly",
  options: {
    allowedTools: ["Read", "Glob", "Grep", "Agent"],
    agents: {
      "code-reviewer": {
        description: "Expert code reviewer for quality, security, and performance",
        prompt: `You are a senior code reviewer. Analyze for:
          - Security vulnerabilities
          - Performance issues
          - Code quality and maintainability
          - Testing coverage
          Return structured feedback with severity levels.`,
        tools: ["Read", "Glob", "Grep"],
        model: "claude-opus-4-1"  // Use best model for reviews
      }
    }
  }
})) {
  console.log(message);
}
```

#### Migration Checklist

1. **Extract skill descriptions** from your SKILL.md `description` fields
2. **Map to SDK configuration:**
   - Simple skills → Keep as `.claude/skills/` and use `settingSources: ['project']`
   - Complex skills → Convert to `agents` definition for more control
3. **Update tool permissions:**
   - Old: `allowed-tools` in SKILL.md
   - New: `allowedTools` in query options
4. **Test in SDK** before deploying:
   ```python
   options = ClaudeAgentOptions(
       cwd="/path/to/project",
       setting_sources=["project"],
       allowed_tools=["Skill", "Read", "Edit", "Bash"]
   )
   ```

#### Example: Converting a "Code Review" Skill

**Original Claude Code skill (.claude/skills/code-reviewer/SKILL.md):**
```markdown
---
description: Review code for quality, security, and best practices
allowed-tools: [Read, Glob, Grep]
---

# Code Reviewer

You are a senior code reviewer. Analyze files for:
- Security vulnerabilities
- Performance issues
- Best practice violations
```

**As SDK subagent:**
```python
options = ClaudeAgentOptions(
    allowed_tools=["Read", "Glob", "Grep", "Agent"],
    agents={
        "code-reviewer": AgentDefinition(
            description="Expert code reviewer for quality and security",
            prompt="Review code for vulnerabilities, performance, and best practices",
            tools=["Read", "Glob", "Grep"]
        )
    }
)
```

---

### Key Resources

**Official Documentation:**
- [Agent SDK Overview](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/overview)
- [Quickstart Guide](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/quickstart)
- [Hosting & Deployment](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/hosting)
- [Skills in SDK](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/skills)
- [TypeScript SDK Reference](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/typescript)
- [Python SDK Reference](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/python)
- [Migration Guide](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/migration-guide)

**GitHub Repositories:**
- [TypeScript SDK](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-agent-sdk-typescript)
- [Python SDK](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-agent-sdk-python)
- [Example Agents](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-agent-sdk-demos) (email assistant, research agent, etc.)

**Learning Resources:**
- [Complete Guide to Building Agents](https://nader.substack.com/p/the-complete-guide-to-building-agents)
- [SerpAPI Tutorial (April 2026)](https://serpapi.com/blog/build-an-ai-agent-with-claude-agent-sdk/)
- [Context Studios Beginner's Guide](https://www.contextstudios.ai/blog/claude-code-agent-sdk-the-complete-beginners-guide-to-building-ai-agents-2026)

---

### Summary: Design a Translation from Claude Code to Agent SDK

1. **Keep your Skills as-is** if they work in Claude Code—they translate directly
2. **For deployment:**
   - Run SDK in containers (ephemeral or persistent based on use case)
   - Set `settingSources: ['project']` to load your `.claude/skills/` directory
   - Enable `"Skill"` tool in `allowedTools`
   - Control permissions via main `allowedTools` config, not SKILL.md frontmatter
3. **For complex/specialized agents:**
   - Convert high-value skills to programmatic subagents
   - Tailor models, prompts, and tool access per subagent
4. **For web access:**
   - Wrap `query()` in HTTP endpoints
   - Manage authentication, isolation, and cost
   - Use sandbox providers for production security

The beauty of this approach is that your existing Claude Code skills become reusable across CLI and deployed SDK agents with minimal changes.

Sources:
- [Agent SDK Overview](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/overview)
- [Agent SDK Quickstart](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/quickstart)
- [Hosting the Agent SDK](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/hosting)
- [Agent Skills in the SDK](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/skills)
- [TypeScript SDK Reference](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/typescript)
- [Migrate to Claude Agent SDK](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/migration-guide)
- [The Complete Guide to Building Agents with the Claude Agent SDK](https://nader.substack.com/p/the-complete-guide-to-building-agents)
- [GitHub: claude-agent-sdk-typescript](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-agent-sdk-typescript)
- [GitHub: claude-agent-sdk-python](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-agent-sdk-python)

content.text:
Good. I now have a clear picture of your prototype and the Agent SDK landscape. Let me summarize what I see, then ask my first clarifying question.

**What you have:** A well-designed "Mindful Consumption Agent" with 5 Claude Code skills — a session router, Socratic want-examination, ad/persuasion reframing, flourishing exercises, and gratitude inventory. These skills chain together naturally (e.g., want-examination can route to reframe). The agent has a warm-coach personality, strong "do not" guardrails, and test personas.

**What you want:** Translate this into a deployable agent via the Claude Agent SDK so someone can follow a web link and interact with it.

**What I learned about the Agent SDK:** It provides a `query()` function (Python or TypeScript) that gives you Claude Code's autonomous capabilities as a library. Existing `.claude/skills/` can potentially be loaded directly. It needs container hosting for deployment and can be wrapped in HTTP endpoints for web access.

---

Here's my first question:

**Who is the target audience for the deployed agent, and what should their experience look like?**

Specifically:
- **(A)** Students in your class deploying their own agents as a learning exercise (each team deploys their prototype)
- **(B)** A single deployed instance of the Mindful Consumption example agent that students or anyone can try via a web link (demo/showcase)
- **(C)** A reusable deployment template that any of the 12 project ideas could be deployed with (generic scaffold)
- **(D)** Something else?

This determines whether we're building a one-off deployment, a template, or deployment infrastructure.
B and C

content.thinking:
The user wants both B and C:
- B: A single deployed instance of the Mindful Consumption example agent that people can try via a web link
- C: A reusable deployment template that any of the 12 project ideas could be deployed with

This is good - the Mindful Consumption agent becomes the example deployment, and the scaffold is reusable for other projects.

Next clarifying question - I should ask about the tech stack / deployment preferences.

content.text:
Great — so the Mindful Consumption agent serves as the working example, and the deployment scaffold is reusable for any of the 12 project ideas. That's a clean design: build the template, prove it with your example agent, then students follow the same pattern.

Next question:

**What's your preference for the web interface users would interact with?**

- **(A)** A simple chat UI (text input, message bubbles, minimal styling) — think a clean single-page app that just works
- **(B)** Something more polished with branding — agent name, description, maybe the persona selector built in so users can try different scenarios
- **(C)** As minimal as possible — even a terminal/console-style interface is fine, the point is that it's accessible via URL
- **(D)** You'd like it to feel like a real product prototype — something you could show to someone outside the class and they'd understand what it is without explanation
B and they can edit information about themselves (their own persona) and upload files etc the agent can access

content.thinking:
Interesting - so they want:
- A polished chat UI with branding (agent name, description)
- Persona selector to try different scenarios
- Users can edit their own persona information
- Users can upload files that the agent can access

This adds some real product-like features. Let me ask about the deployment infrastructure/hosting next.

content.text:
That's a rich feature set — a branded chat interface where users can customize their persona profile and upload files the agent can reference during conversations. This starts to feel like a real product prototype, not just a demo.

Next question:

**What hosting/infrastructure constraints should we design around?**

- **(A)** University-provided infrastructure (do you have access to servers, cloud credits, etc.?)
- **(B)** Free-tier cloud services (e.g., Vercel, Fly.io, Railway free tiers) — needs to work within free limits
- **(C)** Paid cloud is fine — you have Anthropic API credits and are willing to spend modest amounts on hosting (e.g., $5-20/month)
- **(D)** It should run locally on a student's machine and be exposed via something like ngrok or a tunnel, so there's no hosting cost
- **(E)** Some combination — what matters most is that it's easy for students to replicate
C but we will eventually do something more like B

content.thinking:
So paid cloud is fine for now, but eventually they want to move to free-tier. That means we should design something that's lightweight and could potentially run on free tiers, even if for now they'll pay modest amounts.

Next question - I should ask about Python vs TypeScript, since the Agent SDK supports both.

content.text:
Good — design for paid cloud now, but keep it lightweight enough that it can migrate to free tiers later. That means we should avoid heavy infrastructure dependencies.

Next question:

**Python or TypeScript for the backend?**

The Agent SDK supports both. Considerations:

- **(A)** Python — your existing eval code is Python, students likely more comfortable with it, and the course materials reference Python
- **(B)** TypeScript — better ecosystem for web apps (frontend + backend in one language), easier deployment on platforms like Vercel
- **(C)** Don't care — pick whatever makes the template simplest for students to understand and modify

lastPrompt: C but we will eventually do something more like B

Python it is — consistent with your eval code and more accessible for students.

Next question:

**How should the agent handle conversation state and user data?**

Your current skills have transition points (e.g., want-examination can route to reframe, any path can close with gratitude-inventory). In a deployed web app, we need to decide:

- **(A)** Conversations are ephemeral — once the user closes the tab, the conversation is gone. User persona/profile is saved, but chat history is not.
- **(B)** Conversations persist across sessions — users can come back and continue where they left off, or review past conversations
- **(C)** Start simple with ephemeral conversations, but design the data model so persistence can be added later
C

Smart — keep the MVP simple but don't paint ourselves into a corner.

Next question:

**How should authentication work? Who can access the deployed agent?**

- **(A)** No auth — anyone with the link can use it (simplest, fine for a demo/class context)
- **(B)** Simple shared password or access code — keep random internet traffic out but don't need individual accounts
- **(C)** Individual user accounts (email/password or OAuth) — needed if you want per-user persona profiles to persist
- **(D)** Start with A or B, but design so C can be added later
A

Good — open access keeps the barrier low for demos and class use.

One more question, then I think I have enough to propose approaches:

**What's the relationship between the skill prompts and the deployed agent's behavior?**

Your current skills are rich markdown documents with detailed instructions, tone guidance, question flows, transition points, and critical rules. In the Agent SDK, we need to decide how these become the agent's brain:

- **(A)** Translate the skills into a single consolidated system prompt — one big prompt that captures the router logic, all 5 skill behaviors, tone, and guardrails
- **(B)** Keep the skills as separate documents the agent loads dynamically at runtime (closer to how Claude Code discovers them now)
- **(C)** Hybrid — a core system prompt with the personality/principles, and the individual skills loaded as reference documents the agent can consult based on the conversation flow
- **(D)** Whatever approach makes it easiest for students to modify — they should be able to edit a skill markdown file and see the behavior change without touching code

You should just use the typical patterns used when using the claude agents sdk, which I believe allow for skills to be just directly included in the agent, with some way to always start by running the session-start skill

content.thinking:
The user wants me to use the standard Claude Agent SDK patterns for including skills. They believe the SDK has a way to include skills directly and have the agent always start with the session-start skill.

Let me verify this by looking more carefully at what the research agent found. The research mentioned:
- Skills can be loaded via `settingSources: ['project']` which loads `.claude/skills/`
- Adding `"Skill"` to `allowedTools`
- Claude auto-discovers and invokes matching skills

But I should also check - does the Agent SDK actually support skills natively? The research agent may have been extrapolating. Let me do some more targeted research to verify the actual SDK capabilities and typical patterns.


Looking back at the research, it does say the Agent SDK supports the same SKILL.md format with `settingSources: ['user', 'project']` and adding `"Skill"` to `allowedTools` for auto-invocation. However, I need to be cautious about whether the research agent hallucinated these details, since the Agent SDK is relatively new. The user seems familiar with it, so I should verify what they actually know versus what might be inaccurate.

I think the core approach is that the Agent SDK wraps Claude Code as a subprocess, skills from `.claude/skills/` would be discoverable if they're in the working directory, and the system prompt can instruct the agent to start with session-start. Rather than diving deeper into verification, I should move forward with what I understand: the user wants to deploy the Mindful Consumption agent as a reusable template with a polished chat UI supporting persona editing and file uploads, backed by Python on a modest paid cloud tier eventually offering a free tier.

Now I'm ready to outline the actual implementation approaches—I have the key requirements locked in: ephemeral conversations with a data model ready for persistence, open access without auth, and standard Agent SDK skill patterns using session-start as the entry point.

content.text:
That makes sense — use the SDK's native skill support rather than reinventing the wheel. The Agent SDK can load `.claude/skills/` directly, and the system prompt can instruct it to always invoke session-start first.

I think I have enough to propose approaches now. Let me summarize what I've learned, then present options.

---

**What we're building:**
- A deployed Mindful Consumption Agent accessible via web link, using the Claude Agent SDK
- Python backend, polished chat UI with persona editing and file uploads
- Reusable template any of the 12 project ideas can follow
- Paid cloud hosting (modest), designed to migrate to free tiers later
- Ephemeral conversations (persistence-ready data model), no auth
- Skills loaded natively via the SDK, session-start always fires first

---

Here are three approaches, with trade-offs:

### Approach A: Agent SDK + FastAPI + Simple React Frontend

**Architecture:** Python FastAPI server wraps the Claude Agent SDK `query()` call. A React (or plain HTML/JS) frontend provides the chat UI. Skills live in `.claude/skills/` as-is. Deployed as a single Docker container on Fly.io or Railway.

- **Backend:** FastAPI with WebSocket for streaming agent responses
- **Frontend:** Lightweight React app (or even vanilla JS) with chat bubbles, persona editor panel, file upload
- **Skills:** Loaded natively by the SDK from the project directory
- **Deployment:** Single Dockerfile, deploy to Fly.io (~$3-5/month)

**Pros:** Simple architecture, one container, students only need to understand FastAPI + basic frontend. Easy to modify skills by editing markdown files.

**Cons:** WebSocket streaming adds some complexity. React adds a build step students need to understand.

### Approach B: Agent SDK + Flask + HTMX Frontend (No Build Step)

**Architecture:** Same backend concept but with Flask (simpler than FastAPI for students) and HTMX for the frontend — no JavaScript build step, no npm, no React. Server-rendered HTML with HTMX for dynamic updates.

- **Backend:** Flask with Server-Sent Events (SSE) for streaming
- **Frontend:** Jinja2 templates + HTMX + minimal CSS. No build tools.
- **Skills:** Same native SDK loading
- **Deployment:** Same single Dockerfile on Fly.io

**Pros:** Dramatically simpler for students — no frontend build pipeline, no node_modules, no React concepts. The entire app is Python + HTML. Easier to understand and modify. Lower barrier to reuse for the 12 project ideas.

**Cons:** Less polished feel than React. HTMX streaming is less smooth than WebSocket. Persona editor and file upload will require more manual HTML work.

### Approach C: Agent SDK + Streamlit (Minimal Custom Code)

**Architecture:** Use Streamlit as the web framework — it handles the UI, state, file uploads, and user input with almost no frontend code. The Agent SDK runs behind Streamlit's chat interface.

- **Backend:** Streamlit app calling the Agent SDK
- **Frontend:** Streamlit's built-in chat UI, sidebar for persona editing, native file upload widget
- **Skills:** Same native SDK loading
- **Deployment:** Streamlit Community Cloud (free) or Docker on Fly.io

**Pros:** Least code to write. Students already know Python — no HTML/CSS/JS at all. Built-in chat component, file upload, sidebar. Free hosting on Streamlit Cloud. Fastest path to a working demo.

**Cons:** Less control over UI polish/branding. Streamlit's execution model (reruns the whole script on each interaction) can be confusing. Harder to customize beyond what Streamlit provides. May feel like a data-science demo rather than a product prototype.

---

**My recommendation: Approach B (Flask + HTMX)**

It hits the sweet spot for your goals:
- Students only need Python + HTML skills (no JavaScript framework)
- Clean enough for a branded, polished feel (with some good CSS)
- Simple enough to be a reusable template across all 12 projects
- No build step means students can modify and redeploy quickly
- Flask is widely taught and well-documented

Approach A is better if you want maximum UI polish. Approach C is better if you want minimum code. But B balances simplicity, customizability, and professional feel.
